What Is Everything Made Of?

# What Is Everything Made Of?

[](https://substack.com/@jamvasu)

![Srinivas Jammalamadaka's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!r4yD!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8e86879b-57b7-418c-8baf-81252d34c6c4_96x96.png)

[Srinivas Jammalamadaka](https://substack.com/@jamvasu)

Apr 08, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

5

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!LaM8!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbb6079f6-def4-4e2c-b206-556d1175df52_250x167.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!LaM8!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbb6079f6-def4-4e2c-b206-556d1175df52_250x167.jpeg)

Every civilization has asked it. Every philosophy has attempted it. But very few have built a method — a rigorous, tested, tradition-backed way of arriving at the answer — that has held for millennia without cracking.

Sanātana Dharma has that method. And nowhere is it more visible than in the Brahmasūtras.

Vyāsa Maharṣi composed the Brahmasūtras not as a collection of beliefs to be accepted, but as a systematic investigation — a philosophical audit of the entire body of Vedic teaching. Every claim the śruti makes, every apparent contradiction between passages, every rival interpretation that a sharp mind might raise — Vyāsa meets them all, compresses the resolution into a handful of words, and moves on. The sūtras are dense precisely because they are precise. Nothing is there by accident. Nothing is ornamental.

And then Śaṅkarācārya arrives — and the sūtras open up like a held breath finally released.

The Muṇḍakopaniṣad makes a claim that demands scrutiny. It speaks of a bhūtayoni — the womb of all existence, the ground from which everything arises. It describes this source in language that is almost deliberately disorienting:

Adṛśyam. Agrāhyam. Agotram. Avarṇam.

Imperceptible. Ungraspable. Without lineage. Without form. Eternal. All-pervading. Supremely subtle.

Any honest reader pauses here. This is not a casual description. This is the śruti pointing at something with extraordinary specificity — and that specificity raises an immediate question. Who or what is actually being pointed at?

Three candidates are possible. The jīva — the individual self, the experiencer within each being. The Pradhāna — the primal unconscious substrate that Sāṃkhya proposes as the material ground of everything. Or Parameśvara — the conscious, transcendent, all-pervading reality that the upaniṣads return to again and again.

It is not a small question. Get it wrong, and the entire interpretation of the upaniṣad collapses.

अदृश्यत्वादिगुणको धर्मोक्तेः ॥ २१ ॥

Vyāsa's first sūtra in this adhikaraṇam is almost a challenge thrown back at the questioner: look at the qualities themselves. The tradition's method here is not to appeal to authority or to assert a conclusion. It is to ask — what does the description demand? What kind of entity must this be, given everything the śruti says about it?

Imperceptible to the senses. Beyond all mental grasp. Eternal. All-pervading. Supremely subtle. Place the jīva against this description — the jīva who is caught inside a particular body, operating through a particular mind, conditioned by a particular web of karma and ignorance. Does it fit? Not even close. The jīva is the one searching for the answer. It cannot simultaneously be the ground being described.

The tradition's logic is clean: the description itself is the evidence. Dharma here is being established not by decree, but by reading what is actually said.

विशेषणभेदव्यपदेशाभ्यां च नेतरौ ॥ २२ ॥

But the method doesn't rest with one argument. Śaṅkarācārya, following Vyāsa, tightens the reasoning through what might be called double exclusion — ruling out both remaining candidates through the śruti's own words, on two independent grounds.

The śruti calls this bhūtayoni — divya, amūrta, ajaḥ, aprāṇaḥ, amanaḥ — transcendent, formless, birthless, beyond breath, beyond mind. This is viśeṣaṇa — the qualifying description. The jīva is specifically the one constituted by mind, breath, and the limiting adjuncts of embodied existence. These attributes cannot belong to him.

And then — "akṣarāt parataḥ paraḥ" — beyond even the Akṣara, beyond even the unmanifest seed-state from which creation unfolds. This is bheda-vyapadeśa — explicit differentiation. Whatever Pradhāna might be conceived as, the śruti places the bhūtayoni categorically beyond it. The tradition is not rejecting the Sāṃkhya framework out of rivalry — it is showing, from within the śruti's own language, that the entity being described simply cannot be contained within that framework.

Two instruments of exclusion. Both drawn from the text itself. Neither relying on anything imported from outside.

रूपोपन्यासाच्च ॥ २३ ॥

And then the śruti does something unexpected. After all that negation — imperceptible, formless, beyond mind — it turns around and says: fire is this being's head. The sun and moon are its eyes. Earth is born from its feet. It is sarvabhūtāntarātmā — the inner self of every being that exists.

How do you hold these two descriptions together? Is this contradiction? Inconsistency?

Śaṅkarācārya's answer reveals the depth of the tradition's philosophical sensitivity. This is not a description of a form. It is a declaration of sarvātmatva — total identity with all existence. The Taittirīya Upaniṣad speaks in the same register: "aham annam, aham annādaḥ" — I am food, I am the eater. This is not a literal claim about diet. It is the voice of the one who has recognised themselves as the ground of everything, speaking from that fullness.

The bhūtayoni is not somewhere behind the world. It is present as the world — holding it, pervading it, being its innermost self — while remaining untouched by it. Only Parameśvara, the conscious and infinite ground of existence, can be the inner self of all beings simultaneously. The jīva cannot — it is finite, particular, located. An inert Pradhāna cannot — it has no interiority to offer.

Three sūtras. Three movements of one sustained inquiry. And at the end of it, the tradition arrives — not through faith, not through assertion, but through applied reasoning on the śruti's own testimony — at a conclusion that cannot be dislodged:

The bhūtayoni, the womb of all existence, is Parameśvara. The very one the upaniṣads have been pointing at from the beginning. The sākṣāt aupaniṣada puruṣa.

This is what Sanātana Dharma means when it says it is not a belief system. It is a darśana — a way of seeing, built on the most exacting standards of evidence, inference, and textual reasoning that any civilisation has ever produced.
